raw_consumer_statement:
On XX/XX/year>, I was contacted by someone impersonating my boss who urgently requested {$600.00} worth of gift cards, claiming they were needed for a business reason. I was scheduled to meet my boss at a conference the next day, and he was already supposed to reimburse me for travel, so the request seemed legitimate. I purchased XXXX {$200.00} gift cards and sent the codes, believing the request was real. 

Later that day, I confirmed with another XXXX that my boss never made the request. I had been manipulated through impersonation and social engineering. 

I disputed the charge with American Express and explained the situation. I provided supporting documentation including receipts, scam messages, and an FTC report. Amex initially credited the charge, but later reversed it after receiving standard documentation from the merchant. 

This was not a merchant error the issue is that the charge was made under false pretenses. I did not use the gift cards myself, and the transaction was not legitimate. XXXX requesting a proper review under the Fair Credit Billing Act.
